sentences.text: Pesach was a very special holiday in our house because we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we should fall asleep.
The services were conducted and the songs were sang such a long time that most-- my younger brother and I, we always fell asleep.

But it was a very cheerful type of a holiday, with many songs and asking the four questions, which I always volunteered to be the first one to ask the-- the four questions, which are called the [YIDDISH].
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a very festive holiday in our house.
My mother used to work quite a bit to cook their meals and to prepare the dishes to make them kosher for Pesach, and boil them in water in big pots.
And it was quite a-- a busy time in-- in our home.
But it was also a very special time, a lot of anticipation of Pesach, of the Seder.
And it was a-- It was a long holiday because we used to have two Seders, and then have the [HEBREW].
So it was a festive as well as a religious holiday.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were sitting at the table and all I remember, that took a long time for the Seders to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to complete the Seders.
It used to be very late at night.
So I know that my younger brother definitely, and I myself, fell sleep most of the time at the end.
We never finished it.
So-- but it was a very important holiday [INAUDIBLE] in my home.
My-- my-- well, my grandfather was a widower.
So he would eat a Pesach.
I remember he used to come to us quite often.
And he would go to his daughter's, because we were-- we had-- we lived in the building.
Two of my aunts-- other aunts lived in the building, and his son.

Unlike other holidays which were, like Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, that was-- where we spent a lot of-- a long time in the temple listening to the-- to the cousin and the rabbi speaking and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] waiting for any-- for the [NON-ENGLISH] to [INAUDIBLE] so that we-- the services would be over.
And we had a temple that was-- the men were sitting on the first floor, and the women were sitting on the second floor.
It was like a round-- round dome with balconies.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on Yom Kippur, many people would faint and-- because of the fasting.
It was a very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everyone was at awe, to, and-- and especially on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
It was like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- I don't know how to describe it, but it was like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a special atmosphere.
Like in Hebrew they would say like [HEBREW], [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] a solemn occasion.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we also had very festive and less religious holidays, like Purim, and-- which we used to get-- we used to go and make noises in the temple with the [INAUDIBLE] and we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was too small, but my brothers and my father used to get the [NON-ENGLISH], dancing with the Torah.
And-- and-- and Hanukkah was probably the most enjoyable because we had candles, lit candles every night, and-- and drank.

And we sang the various songs about Hanukkah and about the destruction of the Temple and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the, also, the [INAUDIBLE] how the Miracle of the Lights, how there's a song that describes it, how the candles were burning for eight days without-- without-- actually, not the candles, the oil was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I remember these--
Yeah, I remember.
[SINGING IN HEBREW] We sang various songs.
This one I remember the best.
I would say that in my father-- grandfather's building, there mainly was-- there was Jewish people, mainly, living there.
I don't remember.
We had a caretaker which was non-Jewish.
And he had his own apartment, because of-- he was the custodian, the caretaker.

And also he used to come around on Shabbat and high holidays, and turn on and off the candles, the lights, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and perform some work that was not allowed because of the Shabbat, of the Sabbath.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The family was important in Oswiecim because it was a-- an old, established family.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandmother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her name was-- maiden name was Van [?
Plasner. ?] She-- they were old Austrian Jews with a title.
So she was a contessa, count-- a countess.
Van [?
Plasner, ?] that was her maiden name.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the same person that I describe where people-- you should tell me how they-- they say we need more people like this now to help the poor.
And your grandmother was such a terrific person that she made sure that the poor people had food and-- and were able to keep warm.
And that was her.
You should take the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] help, you know, the service people-- the servants, and-- and help distribute foods and clothes and wood, and mainly a lot of food.
My understanding is that the [GERMAN], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the title was-- they were-- they were from Vienna.
And they-- her father, my grandmother's father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a professor.
And he wrote a-- some special type of [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] books.
And they always used to say how wonderful it was, you know, when-- because there was an expression that the Germans and the Austrians used to use.
Maybe in those days they used to say, 'Leben und leben lassen.' Live and let live.
But this really was not-- I did not experience that.
I experienced just the opposite.
I'm sorry, right.
I don't know exactly.
We had very little to do with the non-Jewish people.
And we really didn't have any neighbors that would live next door.
So all I know, that we know that-- we knew that the antisemitism was always present, especially on Easter time.
We used to close the gate and put the [NON-ENGLISH] on the door, close the-- because they would throw stones.
And Christmas was probably the worst time because they-- the Pollacks truly believed that the Jews killed Christ, they-- that the Jews killed their God.
And the antisemitism in Poland was inbred in-- at-- in home already.
When the chil-- they talked about it, about the dirty Jews at home before the children even started school.

I do remember an incident where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- where a group of children, when I was in school, they-- they were-- they was-- they were-- they got a hold of me and they-- I was hit with a stone in my head.
And I came home all bloody.
My mother almost fainted.
But-- but I knew exactly which guy did it.
And there was maybe four or five of them together.
And I said, I'm not going to stand for it.
So I-- I did seek this guy out separately.
So it was a different situation.
And he really-- he would not participate anymore because, one on one, they were not that brave.
My teacher, I remember was a major in the Polish army.
And all my-- all my siblings went to the same school.
So the parents' conference, they used to go and find out how-- and my-- I can't think of his name, but he would say, [POLISH].
It means, Mrs.
Bader, you should only have this much pleasure from the rest of your children, because [INAUDIBLE] is doing a terrific job.
You know, so-- I used to I used to work at it.
And I-- and my grandfather was-- when we-- when we used to get our report cards, he would-- I would get the biggest amount of the-- whatever.
Someone would get maybe a dollar, maybe I would get $2 because they all knew that I would bring the best report card.
And he would sit in the the main-- inside the square of our building, and the kids would pass by him, and he would reward them for-- for getting-- but he would give each one something, regardless.
I-- I told him that I'm going to beat the hell out of him, and I did, yes.
And I-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and since then, he would not participate.
We had a radio towards the year-- I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the end of-- before the Germans came.
I remember having a radio.
But--
Yes, we-- I did.
And we also had, besides the radio, we had a micro-- a gramophone.
And I used to always, especially during the war where our parents would not-- didn't let the kids go out at night, so friends and relatives would come to our apartment.

And I-- and I would be given five pennies to keep it-- to-- I would crawl under the table and keep the gramophone-- I would wind it up constantly and replace-- and change the plates, the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] record.
Right, yeah.
A lot of-- because they danced and they-- so a lot of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] variety of music, I would say, a lot of the kind of music you can dance to.
And some of it was even classical music, because we had a-- I remember the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one tenor whose name was Yom Kippur.
He was quite-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the young people liked to listen to it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I used to-- I was in charge of the microphone-- of the gramophone.
And we had a little radio which we would also listen to.
Probably more Jewish and whatever they were dancing in those days, maybe tango and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] waltz or polka.
No.
No.
No, that I experienced after.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, we heard on the radio.
We also-- it was written in the papers as well as-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we were concerned about it.
I remember my father talking about that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they felt that, you know, being who they are and where they are, that maybe nothing would happen to them.
That was a big mistake.

That-- because I remember when they were talking about these things and-- and even I-- I used to read the papers quite a bit, whatever paper we had in Oswiecim, because I was fascinated prior to that how, for so many years, the Japanese and the Chinese were fighting, you know, and so many people were killed.
All this was taking place, and the Poles in the parliament were talking about the shechita.
I don't know if you-- the [HEBREW] means how you slaughter animal, like for food, you know.
And they did not-- they were discussing it that the Jews should not-- you cannot shoot the animal.
It has to be slaughtered by a shochet.
And that's what they were occupied with.
Because not too long after that, I was listening to the radio where they were-- where the Germans were getting ready to occupy Poland.
And to-- listening to the radio was like a comedy, that they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] passwords like [POLISH].
And then we could see-- they had little tanks.
We could see the tanks.
They were totally disorganized.
And the horses pulling the-- the-- the cannons.
But they mainly, the little tanks, like maybe one or two man tank, probably one man tank.
And when they would say [POLISH], they would go one direction.
When they would say [POLISH], they would say-- go in the other direction.
It was total chaos.
Yeah, I was 12 years old.
There was anticipation and worries and concern, and a lot of talk about it.
But unfortunately no action.
No one took any action.
Maybe some people did.
The summer of 1939, I remember that the-- Marshal Pilsudski died and the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think  it was 1939.
And they elected a new marshal, which was a protege to-- his name was Smigly-Rydz-- took the place.
And they were singing songs about how they're going to fight the Germans.
And they would take an example from the Smigly-Rydz, they would-- and that they are ready for them, but they were-- really didn't.
The Polish Army was always terrific when it came to prepare a parade because they had those white horses, [INAUDIBLE], pulling the cannons.
But when the German came, the white horses were muddy and couldn't get a-- pull the cannons out of the mud.
I don't think it was any different.
And-- maybe there was more participa-- anticipation of something that is-- that will be happening, of the war, that the war will come.
They did not know exactly when.
But life was normal as always, with the exception of this concern.
When the invasion occurred, we were-- I was-- yes, I remember.
When the invasion occurred, we had-- there was some-- excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were some wagons with troops, Polish troops, that pulled in into-- into our building, to the square of my grandfather's building.
And they locked the gate.
And they took off the Polish uniform, and underneath there was a German uniform.
That's how well they-- I remember this, and I was-- and I saw it with my own eyes.
The Poles never had a chance because they were infiltrated.
Because the area where I was born, that was Silesia, and it was always either Poland or German.
The language was going back and forth.
So they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were Germans that could speak Polish as well as the German language.
And-- and I was amazed when I saw what was happening in a-- in a few hours.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The city was occupied by the Germans.
I don't remember any bombing or destruction or any kind in Oswiecim.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I was-- we locked the door to the apartment, and we stayed inside, and-- waiting to see what will happen next.
What happened next is they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occupied the city real quick.
And about a week later, or two weeks later, they changed the name from-- the main square to Adolf Hitler Square.
And they made us use arm bands with the Star of David, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a yellow band.
And then they changed this.
And later on it was changed where you had to sew up a permanent yellow star and said Jude.
